chapters.title: Judgment is a habit, not a trait
chapters.text:
The final step of the judgment detox lies in your willingness to forgive and let go. Of course, you may struggle to forgive physical or mental abuse or betrayal. You may not forget these traumas, but you can try to accept them and move on with your life. Eventually, you’ll notice your emotions fade when you recall the issue and feel less anger and hate.

The road to forgiveness is a long one, but you can manage it with these steps:

1. Silence your ego when it judges or blames someone. Try to see yourself in another person and recall what you did wrong. For example, recognize your wrongdoings when you read the news and judge someone for stealing. Even if you never stole, think of the situations you could do it for a particular reason, like to feed your children or to survive.
2. Accept your mistake and choose to feel and think differently. You are human, and that is reason enough to judge others. So, accept your weaknesses and treat others with love and care because we all are struggling and need the same — love.
3. Seek and receive help. Asking higher powers to support you in your battles, lead you through dark times, and shed light on your despair will help you overcome even the most challenging moments.

Remember: If we were happy and complete, we wouldn’t judge. ~ Gabrielle Bernstein

Gabrielle Bernstein

When you ask for help in spiritual practice, you open new paths for forgiveness and peace to find you. They can make you speak up against injustice and fight against abuse, but you will do it with confidence and out of love, not out of pain or revenge.

Self-criticism destroys while loving repairs.

Judgment is decisive but does not affect you if you know how to forgive. When you accept others even before they do something wrong, you feel joyful and peaceful whatever happens. For example, little misconceptions or fights will not bother you if you treat everyone with compassion and understanding; after all, everyone makes mistakes.

Demanding justice and seeking revenge are different things.

So, let others be wrong. Forgive them for jumping in line, talking too slowly, or doubting your decisions. That is their choice, and your part is to forgive. Sure, you can oppose them, but don’t let it trouble or break you emotionally or start judging them.

chapters.title: Recognizing your dark side enhances growth
chapters.text:
Many heartbroken or betrayed people distance themselves from others with anger, pain, and hatred. It is a fruitful ground for judgment to silence love and compassion. Even a tiny misconception or difference in views may lead to dramatic outcomes.

Viewing the world through the lens of judgment makes you accept everyone as an enemy even before they approach you. The most obvious example is making assumptions about a person’s looks. For instance, many perceive a person with bright-colored hair and pierced nose as an extremist, outcast, or rascal.

Judgment is the forefather of stereotypes and preconceptions.

The reasons for people’s traumas can be different, but the result is the same. We use judgment to protect ourselves from shame and reliving that hurt again. No matter how controversial it is, you relive that emotion when you push out your claws and prepare for the fight in every situation that triggers your painful memory. For example, you may behave rudely and unethically if your date reminds you of your ex-partner. Thus, judging impedes and deteriorates your personal and spiritual growth, affecting your most beautiful traits.

Judgment may taste sweet but will make you bitter over time.

The only way to fight judgment is to face your darkness and accept it without fear and condemnation. You must observe and catch yourself red-handed, not as a criminal but as an innocent child who made a mistake. Scolding yourself for imperfection will only hinder your progress.

Realizing the root cause of condemnation — the fear of being insufficient or unworthy — will also assist in battling this habit. This fright makes you cross the line and hurt others only because it gives you a sense of protection that no one will discover your self-destructive truth.

You cannot eradicate your dark side as duality makes you human, but it’s essential to recognize and accept it. Understanding the reasons for your judgments will help you to reduce the habit. Here are four aspects you must determine when you are criticizing anyone:

• Who or what does it refer to?
• How does it make you feel?
• How do you justify such behavior?
• What trauma or experience triggered this judgment?

This technique gives results if you do it consistently. Start with writing out at least 15 repeated judgments daily. The deeper you go, the more you will find.

chapters.title: Tap your way through childhood trauma
chapters.text:
After revealing the reasons for judgment, continue the healing by honoring your trauma. You’ll face and accept your wound when you realize what specific situation or grief made you judge in similar circumstances.

The Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT) helps manage your criticism and overcome your trauma. This method comes from China, where people discovered that energy travels the body via circuits called meridians. This discovery became the foundation of acupuncture and acupressure. The latter is the essence of EFT.

EFT restores and balances your body’s energy shattered by painful experiences. In essence, it makes you feel and surrender to the emotion you fear by tapping on meridians. Thus you’ll release the suppressed feelings and imbalanced energy to make space for love and compassion. So how do you do that?

• Choose the Most Pressing Issue (MPI) bothering you. For example, you condemn your parents for divorcing after many years of marriage.
• Rate it on a scale of zero to ten. The more distressing it is, the higher it gets.
• Follow the script, tapping different facial and body parts and saying words out loud.
• The first phase is letting go of your negative emotions. So, you will be tapping and repeating your destructive beliefs.
• Then, you will switch to the positive round introducing new thoughts and patterns.

Your pain makes you focus on others while you must focus on yourself.

Here is one of the possible EFT variations you can try:

1. Choose your MPI.
2. Keep tapping on your eyebrow and saying the MPI: “I judge my parents for divorcing.”
3. Proceed to other circuits like the eye, nose, chin, collarbone, arm, and top of the head.
4. Change your negative feelings and the excuses for judging with every zone. You can say something like:

• “They deserve my judgments!”
• “I can’t forgive them!”
• “This made me upset!”
• “I blame them for their actions!”

5. Say what feels right to live through those negative patterns while tapping on your meridians.
6. Transform your affirmations into positive ones when the pressure and anxiety ebb away. Repeat the following:

• “I release the blame I put on them.”
• “I feel better when I accept their choice.”
• “I love my parents, and I trust their decisions.”
• “Judgment hurts me, while love makes everything perfect.”

7. Tap through the positive statements until you feel relaxed and happy.
8. Now, rate your MPI again and see how much it reduced.

chapters.title: Put some responsibility on divine powers to find and enjoy peace
chapters.text:
Have you ever analyzed your past? Did you notice that some unusual, unpleasant, or even tragic events brought you to something exceptional? For example, people who overcame addiction realized they didn’t appreciate their life enough, or a college drop-out built a successful career. Any decision, even if it seems like a mistake, can bring you to the desired destination.

However, many neglect the benefits because of their decisions' hardships or complications. Second-guessing your choices imprisons you in an infinite judgment loop. You keep obsessing over the arrangement you made a year ago, thus living in the past and missing the precious moments of today. You put the decision you regret on the altar and keep worshiping it by giving it attention and the best of yourself.

You are where your attention and thoughts are.

Therefore, you put too much pressure on your shoulders by believing everything depends on you. The divine powers also have a say in your life; realizing their impact is vital. Thus, put love on the pedestal; believe that a higher power is leading you and that whatever route you choose, you will reach the destination in your time.

Prayer is an effective way to communicate and let go of your judgment circle. Even if you don’t know how to do it, the intention to eliminate your judgment is also practical.

After you intend or pray, notice different signs of guidance pinpointing the right direction. For example, it can be a friend’s words or a sentence in a book. Even a random thought in the shower may be the last piece of your puzzle.

Love is the antidote to judgment. ~ Gabrielle Bernstein

Gabrielle Bernstein

Cutting out judgment may seem like a betrayal of yourself because you have an illusion that you feel safer with this habit. You need to know this offender is wrong because that corresponds to your worldview and beliefs: they are bad, and you are good. Yet, this truth is a trap. Some victims still live in the loop of their painful trauma, and all their days turn into torture.

You receive peace in your life when you project it in your mind.

Indeed, the more tragic your experience, the harder it will be to release the judgment, but you need it to move on and live a happy life.

chapters.title: A clear vision of life is a foundation of happiness
chapters.text:
Judgment often stops you from seeing things and people as they truly are. It changes your perception drastically. For example, you can live in past tragedies and future worries and become blind to the kindness in the people you judge or the advantages of a wrong decision.

As soon as you get rid of judgments, your vision will clear, and you will appreciate things you never noticed. Here are the principal elements to help you see others as they are on the inside:

• Accept people without trying to change anything in their behavior, personality, or worldview. You should recognize and see how they are now, not a year ago, or what they can be later. Love them without judgment. For example, telling a partner they need to change their style or a job means you align your behavior with criticism, not love.
• Love yourself and accept your dark sides. Recognizing yourself with all negativity and mistakes is critical to creating a deep and wholesome personality. Judging every step on your way impedes your growth and happiness, so strive to see the whole picture.

Seeing the good in yourself will help you see the greatness in others.

• Acceptance comes in practice. Write the name of the person you judge and note everything good about them or your experiences with them. You will see that it strengthens or helps you practice a judgment detox.
• The worst things you see in others can be what you deny in yourself. Therefore, you may struggle to accept that you lied or can lie to others if you keep judging your partner for it.
• Notice the light, not the darkness. You alone choose what you see in this world. You can dwell on the negatives and repeat that the world is going to dogs, or you can see how great this life is and how lucky you are to experience it.
• Let compassion soothe your way. When you empathize with someone’s pain, you will let go of their wrongdoings with less effort. Therefore, you start to look deep into the roots of their behavior rather than the outcomes. Darkness births darkness. So, if the person causes you pain, they are suffering from more significant pain.

Judgment obscures your vision.

These tips are vital to make you accept and let go of judgments you feel in your daily life.

chapters.title: Integrate a judgment detox into your day through mental practices
chapters.text:
Reducing judgment requires more than intention. You must make it your priority and an essential part of life with the help of the following techniques:

Meditate to let go.

1. Sit in a comfortable and silent place.
2. Close your eyes.
3. Breathe in and out a few times until your mind settles.
4. Imagine a thin line connecting you and the negative feelings.
5. Inhale and exhale, feeling your emotions leave you.
6. Imagine a sword in your hand and cut the cord.
7. Envision the person you judge and try to see them anew, in their innocence and peace.
8. Breathe in and out slowly and steadily.
9. Open your eyes when you feel calm and joyful.

Meditate to forgive.

1. Follow the first steps from the previous meditation: breathe and calm your mind.
2. Imagine a person you judge in front of you.
3. Repeat the following affirmation: “I forgive you.”
4. Say this mantra until you feel anger or hate fading away.
5. You may not forgive on the first try, but the more you do it, the easier it will be.
6. Move out of the meditation when you are ready.

Practice mantra meditation to calm your mind.

1. Close your eyes and focus on the space between your eyebrows.
2. Breathe to calm down.
3. Repeat the mantra: “Sat Nam.” It means: “Truth is my name.”
4. Your thoughts may wander when you repeat the word, so gently return to your focus.
5. Stick with it for twenty minutes.
6. Move out of the meditation slowly.
7. Don’t rush to your routine or phone right after the reflection; spend a few more minutes reflecting and relaxing.

The noise of the world blocks you from enjoying its pleasures.

Meditation is an excellent way to make space for light and joy in your life. You can try different mantras and find ways to picture the person you judge. Be gentle to yourself, and don’t stress out if your thoughts and emotions spin out of control.

Meditate after you wake up to set yourself in the right mood.

Finally, remember to write down your thoughts after each meditation. Write whatever comes to your mind; make your emotions and pain spill onto the paper until you feel relieved. It can help you discover the root of your problem and let it go.

Did you know? According to a Disturbemenot article from January 2022, meditation improves anxiety levels 60% of the time and can increase employee productivity by 120%.

chapters.title: Pave your way to happiness with forgiveness
chapters.text:
The final step of the judgment detox lies in your willingness to forgive and let go. Of course, you may struggle to forgive physical or mental abuse or betrayal. You may not forget these traumas, but you can try to accept them and move on with your life. Eventually, you’ll notice your emotions fade when you recall the issue and feel less anger and hate.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
People get judgmental when something doesn’t go as they want or someone disagrees with their choices. You can blame others for eating meat, having the wrong friends, or letting their children play in your front yard. Or, you can despise some individuals for betraying, neglecting, or leaving you. Your loved ones can enter both categories, creating a distance and deteriorating relationships. Things can get even worse when you dwell on those issues for years, forcing you further into the prison of anxiety and hurt.

Overcoming judgment is challenging because it covers your pain and trauma and imposes a sense of safety. However, realizing it’s only an illusion will make you see others and your experience anew. Very often, you judge others for the sides you despise in yourself. For example, you can scold your partner for being late. Yet, you are so terrified of it that you feel anxious when in traffic or in unpleasant situations that happen out of nowhere.

Therefore, forgive yourself for judging and recognize everyone and yourself for who you are. Practice forgiveness and acceptance through prayer and meditation. Build your routines around the judgment detox tips you have learned, and stay consistent. This path will eventually bring you the peace and joy you have always desired.

Try this

Envision the person you condemn. Think about ways that person is similar to you. The answers may come immediately or over time. Intend to find all the similarities between you. Write them down and meditate or pray to accept your dark sides.

Read it till the end?
